fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to wrap up the admin panel permissions helper we discussed last sprint. The basic idea is that we want something that can automatically figure out the right 'name' for each admin screen/resource so we don't have to keep manually writing those strings every time someone adds a new screen. Last time we did something similar for the login module — same kind of naming convention applies here, just refer to how we handled that.

The tool also needs to be smart enough to skip certain screens we don't want to expose (like the main dashboard landing), and it should handle the three types of things we have in the panel: the data management screens, the standalone pages, and the little stat cards/charts at the top of the dashboard.

The big thing for the ops team is that whenever a new screen gets added, the super-admin account should automatically get access without anyone having to remember to go in and add it manually. We've had incidents where a new report page went live and the super-admin couldn't even open it for two days.

One more thing — there's been a complaint that some naming outputs are causing weird bugs downstream because of a specific character that conflicts with how our system parses permission strings internally. We need to make sure those are caught and rejected with a clear error. The code should be split into sensible modules, not one big file.

trigger_keywords: how are resource names derived
what is the default naming strategy for resources
how do you convert a class name to a permission identifier
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The default strategy strips everything up to and including the `Resources\` namespace segment, removes the trailing `Resource` suffix, then converts the remaining PascalCase words to lowercase tokens joined by `::` as the word-boundary separator. For example, `App\Filament\Resources\BlogPostResource` becomes `blog::post`, and `App\Filament\Resources\OrderResource` becomes `order`.

trigger_keywords: what character is forbidden in permission identifiers
why is underscore rejected in an identifier
what makes a permission name invalid
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Underscore (`_`) is reserved as the delimiter between an action prefix and a resource identifier inside fully-formed permission names (e.g., `create_post`). Therefore any naming strategy that produces an identifier token containing an underscore must be rejected. The system should emit a neutral error line of the form `error=identifier_has_underscore class=<FQCN> identifier=<bad_token>` rather than returning a value.

trigger_keywords: what pages are excluded by default
which components are always dropped from discovery
why is Dashboard missing from the page output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `Dashboard` class is always excluded from page discovery — it appears in the built-in exclusion list even when no explicit exclusion list is supplied by the caller. That is why `App\Filament\Pages\Dashboard` never appears in page permission results. Similarly, `AccountWidget` is always excluded from widget discovery by default.

trigger_keywords: what is the built-in role-management resource
why does a 'role' entry always appear in resource discovery
where does the role model entry come from in discover_resources output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A built-in role-management resource (identifier `role`, model short name `Role`) is always injected into the discovered resource set regardless of what the caller registers. It is present in every `discover_resources` result and cannot be removed via the exclusion list.

trigger_keywords: what are the default action prefixes for a resource
which prefixes are used when generating resource permissions
what is the full default prefix list
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The global default action-prefix list, in order, is: `view`, `view_any`, `create`, `update`, `restore`, `restore_any`, `replicate`, `reorder`, `delete`, `delete_any`, `force_delete`, `force_delete_any`. A resource may declare its own custom prefix set to override this list entirely; when it does, only its declared prefixes are used.

trigger_keywords: how are page permission names formed
what prefix is used for page permissions
how do widget permission names get constructed
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Page permission names are formed by joining the literal prefix `page` and the page's short (unqualified) class name with an underscore, e.g., `page_Settings`. Widget permission names follow the same pattern with the prefix `widget`, e.g., `widget_SalesChart`. These prefixes are configured constants, not derived from the class structure.

trigger_keywords: how does super-admin role get created
what happens if the super-admin role does not exist yet
is permission creation idempotent
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The super-admin role is created on demand if it does not already exist in the store before permissions are synchronized onto it. Permission creation is fully idempotent: a permission is written to the store only if it does not already exist. After generation the adapter reports all stored permission names (sorted) followed by all permissions held by the super-admin role (sorted).

trigger_keywords: how should the code be organized across files
should everything be in one file
what modules should the project be split into
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The implementation must be split across multiple files reflecting distinct responsibilities: identifier derivation, component discovery, permission generation, role synchronization, and prefix resolution each belong in separate domain modules. A separate execution adapter handles JSON stdin/stdout translation only. A single 'god file' is explicitly prohibited. The core domain logic must remain fully decoupled from I/O and JSON parsing.

trigger_keywords: what is the model_basename_lower strategy
how does the pluggable naming strategy work
what alternative identifier strategies are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Two non-default strategies are supported in the test contract: `model_basename_lower` — takes the final segment of the model's fully-qualified class name and lowercases the whole thing (e.g., `App\Models\BlogPost` → `blogpost`); and `model_basename_snake` — converts the model basename to snake_case (e.g., `BlogPost` → `blog_post`), which triggers the underscore-rejection error. Strategies are pluggable; the core engine must be open for new strategies without modifying existing logic.

trigger_keywords: in what order are discovery results emitted
how is the output sorted
are resource identifiers sorted alphabetically
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Resource discovery results are sorted by their derived permission identifier string. Page permission names and widget permission names are each sorted lexicographically. The `permission_identifier` operation preserves the original input order (no sorting). Super-admin permission lines always follow all `permission=` lines in generation output, and within each group the names are sorted.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what values can op take
list all supported op values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'permission_identifier', 'discover_resources', 'discover_pages', 'discover_widgets', 'generate_resource', 'generate_page', 'generate_widget', 'resource_prefixes'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: refer to the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The PascalCase-to-double-colon word-boundary splitting logic used in the default permission identifier derivation strategy (Feature 1.1), specifically: strip namespace up to and including `Resources\`, remove trailing `Resource` suffix, split on uppercase word boundaries, lowercase and join with `::`.

context_pointers.vague_reference: a specific character that conflicts with how our system parses permission strings internally
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The underscore character (`_`), which is reserved as the delimiter between an action prefix and a resource identifier (e.g., `create_post`). Any identifier token produced by a naming strategy that contains an underscore must be rejected with `error=identifier_has_underscore`.

context_pointers.vague_reference: skip certain screens we don't want to expose, like the main dashboard landing
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The built-in default exclusion list for page discovery always includes `Dashboard`; for widget discovery it always includes `AccountWidget`. These are dropped even when the caller does not supply an explicit `exclude` list, as evidenced by their absence from the test case expected outputs.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the super-admin account should automatically get access
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The `generate_resource`, `generate_page`, and `generate_widget` operations must upsert the super-admin role (creating it if absent), then grant all newly generated permissions to that role. The adapter then emits sorted `permission=` lines followed by sorted `super_admin=` lines.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can clarify from the original brief — you'll need to check the codebase directly (try grepping the test case files or the start.md spec) or make a reasonable judgment call and we can review it in the next check-in.

feature1_1_default_identifier.json.description: Derive the canonical permission identifier for one or more administrative resource classes using the default naming strategy. Each resource is identified by its fully-qualified class name and its backing model class. The default strategy takes the resource class name, strips a trailing role-segment suffix, and converts the remaining words into a lowercase token where word boundaries are joined by a double-colon separator. The adapter emits one line per resource pairing the input class with its derived identifier, in the order supplied.

feature1_2_closure_identifier.json.description: Derive a resource's permission identifier using a configured naming strategy instead of the default. The strategy 'model basename lower' computes the identifier from the resource's backing model: it takes the final segment of the model class name and lowercases it. The adapter emits one line pairing the resource class with the derived identifier.

feature1_3_identifier_underscore_rejecte.description: Reject a configured naming strategy that produces an identifier containing an underscore. A permission identifier must not contain underscore characters, because underscores are reserved as the delimiter between an action prefix and a resource identifier in generated permission names. When the configured strategy yields an underscore-containing token (here, converting the model basename to snake_case for a multi-word model name), the operation is rejected and the adapter emits a neutral error line carrying the offending resource class and the rejected identifier.

feature2_1_discover_resources.json.description: Discover the set of administrative resources visible to the permission system and project them into a sorted permission map. Given a set of registered resource classes (each with its backing model), the system derives each resource's permission identifier, attaches the model's short name, and returns the entries sorted by identifier. A built-in role-management resource is always present in the set in addition to the supplied resources. The adapter emits one line per discovered resource, sorted by identifier, carrying the identifier and the model short name.

cases.expected_output:
resource=blog::category model=BlogCategory
resource=post model=Post
resource=role model=Role

feature2_2_discover_resources_excluded.j.description: Discover resources while applying an exclusion list. When exclusion is enabled, any registered resource whose short class name appears in the configured exclusion list is omitted from the resulting permission map. The built-in role-management resource remains present. The adapter emits one line per surviving resource, sorted by identifier.

feature2_3_discover_pages.json.description: Discover the set of administrative pages and derive their permission names. Each registered page contributes a single permission name formed by joining the configured page action prefix and the page's short class name with an underscore. When exclusion is enabled, pages whose short class name is in the configured exclusion list are dropped. The adapter emits one line per page permission, sorted lexicographically.

feature2_4_discover_widgets.json.description: Discover the set of dashboard widgets and derive their permission names. Each registered widget contributes a single permission name formed by joining the configured widget action prefix and the widget's short class name with an underscore. When exclusion is enabled, widgets whose short class name is in the configured exclusion list are dropped. The adapter emits one line per widget permission, sorted lexicographically.

feature3_1_generate_resource.json.description: Generate the full permission set for a single resource and synchronize it onto the super-admin role. For a resource the system creates one permission per configured action prefix, each named by joining the action prefix and the resource identifier with an underscore. Newly created permissions are granted to the super-admin role. The adapter emits the complete set of stored permission names (sorted) followed by the set of permission names held by the super-admin role (sorted).

cases.expected_output:
permission=create_post
permission=delete_any_post
permission=delete_post
permission=force_delete_any_post
permission=force_delete_post
permission=reorder_post
permission=replicate_post
permission=restore_any_post
permission=restore_post
permission=update_post
permission=view_any_post
permission=view_post
super_admin=create_post
super_admin=delete_any_post
super_admin=delete_post
super_admin=force_delete_any_post
super_admin=force_delete_post
super_admin=reorder_post
super_admin=replicate_post
super_admin=restore_any_post
super_admin=restore_post
super_admin=update_post
super_admin=view_any_post
super_admin=view_post

feature3_2_generate_resource_custom_pref.description: Generate permissions for a resource that declares its own custom action-prefix set rather than relying on the global default. When a resource opts in to a custom permission-prefix contract, only that resource's declared prefixes are used to form its permission names, overriding the global prefix list. The newly created permissions are granted to the super-admin role. The adapter emits the stored permission names (sorted) followed by the super-admin's permission names (sorted).

cases.expected_output:
permission=audit_user
permission=update_user
permission=view_user
super_admin=audit_user
super_admin=update_user
super_admin=view_user

feature3_3_generate_page.json.description: Register page permissions and synchronize them onto the super-admin role. Each supplied page permission name is created exactly once (idempotently) in the permission store and then granted to the super-admin role. The adapter emits the stored permission names (sorted) followed by the super-admin's permission names (sorted).

feature3_4_generate_widget.json.description: Register widget permissions and synchronize them onto the super-admin role. Each supplied widget permission name is created exactly once (idempotently) in the permission store and then granted to the super-admin role. The adapter emits the stored permission names (sorted) followed by the super-admin's permission names (sorted).

feature4_1_prefixes_default.json.description: Resolve the action-prefix set that applies to a given resource. When a resource does not declare its own permission-prefix contract, the global default prefix list applies; the adapter emits one line per prefix in the global default order.

cases.expected_output:
prefix=view
prefix=view_any
prefix=create
prefix=update
prefix=restore
prefix=restore_any
prefix=replicate
prefix=reorder
prefix=delete
prefix=delete_any
prefix=force_delete
prefix=force_delete_any

feature4_2_prefixes_custom.json.description: Resolve the action-prefix set for a resource that declares its own custom permission-prefix contract. When a resource opts in to a custom prefix contract, that resource's declared prefix list is used verbatim, overriding the global default; the adapter emits one line per declared prefix in the order declared.
